name: Modelling and simulation of gas permeation

origin.filename: Modelling and simulation of gas permeation.pdf

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Modelling and simulation using Pro/II® 9.3 of gas permeation
texts.text: Modelling and simulation using Pro/II® 9.3 of gas permeation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2 is commonly found in natural gas and must be removed to meet specifications before the gas can be delivered to the pipeline. Among the conventional options of natural gas treatment (e.g. physical or chemical absorption) membrane separation is interesting due to its modularity, compactness and environmental friendliness [1].
texts.text: CO2 is commonly found in natural gas and must be removed to meet specifications before the gas can be delivered to the pipeline. Among the conventional options of natural gas treatment (e.g. physical or chemical absorption) membrane separation is interesting due to its modularity, compactness and environmental friendliness [1].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In membrane separation processes one can distinguish several variables in two different scales: in  a  micro-scale  describing  the  phenomena  related  to  mass  and  energy  transfer  through  the membrane (e.g. sorption and diffusion into membranes, enthalpy change, pressure variation, local composition, etc.) and in a meso-scale describing the characteristics of the gas treatment (e.g. membrane surface, number of stages, flow mode, etc.). As expected, all variation in the microscale will influence the variables of the meso-scale. However, this affectation is sometimes not obvious. Indeed, no general pattern can be established since every answer is system dependent.
texts.text: In membrane separation processes one can distinguish several variables in two different scales: in  a  micro-scale  describing  the  phenomena  related  to  mass  and  energy  transfer  through  the membrane (e.g. sorption and diffusion into membranes, enthalpy change, pressure variation, local composition, etc.) and in a meso-scale describing the characteristics of the gas treatment (e.g. membrane surface, number of stages, flow mode, etc.). As expected, all variation in the microscale will influence the variables of the meso-scale. However, this affectation is sometimes not obvious. Indeed, no general pattern can be established since every answer is system dependent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Developing a simulation tool allowing to relate both scales is therefore of vital importance, since it would provide valuable information about process design and/or equipment size for several membrane  systems.  The  modelling  approaches  of  membrane  separation  and  their  numerical implementation and resolution are explained in Section 2 and Section 3, respectively. Finally, in Section 4 some perspectives about the simulation stability and model enhancement are discussed.
texts.text: Developing a simulation tool allowing to relate both scales is therefore of vital importance, since it would provide valuable information about process design and/or equipment size for several membrane  systems.  The  modelling  approaches  of  membrane  separation  and  their  numerical implementation and resolution are explained in Section 2 and Section 3, respectively. Finally, in Section 4 some perspectives about the simulation stability and model enhancement are discussed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas separation processes are generally considered to be isothermal [2]. In fact, under commonly applied laboratory scale operating conditions, with low stage cut, small membrane modules  and  temperature-controlled  environment,  this  assumption  is  valid.  Furthermore, analysing experimental results and/or performing conceptual engineering is considerably simpler to  conduct.  However,  for  membrane  natural  gas  treatment,  temperature  variations  inside  a membrane module may occur because of mass-transfer due to a relatively high pressure difference applied in the system. Given that almost all physicochemical and thermodynamic properties of fluids and membranes are temperature dependent, the thermal effects are expected to influence the performance of the operation. In this section, both isothermal and adiabatic modelling for membrane gas separation are presented.
texts.text: Membrane gas separation processes are generally considered to be isothermal [2]. In fact, under commonly applied laboratory scale operating conditions, with low stage cut, small membrane modules  and  temperature-controlled  environment,  this  assumption  is  valid.  Furthermore, analysing experimental results and/or performing conceptual engineering is considerably simpler to  conduct.  However,  for  membrane  natural  gas  treatment,  temperature  variations  inside  a membrane module may occur because of mass-transfer due to a relatively high pressure difference applied in the system. Given that almost all physicochemical and thermodynamic properties of fluids and membranes are temperature dependent, the thermal effects are expected to influence the performance of the operation. In this section, both isothermal and adiabatic modelling for membrane gas separation are presented.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane may be implemented in the module as a flat or spiral wound sheet or as a bundle of  hollow  fibres.  The  latter  arrangement  is  preferred  as  it  permits  to  develop  the  highest interfacial  areas  per  unit  volume.  An  industrial  hollow  fibre  membrane  module  contains thousands of fibres grouped in the same bundle, using a grid to maintain the bundle structure. A canal is usually placed in the central axis of the module to function as collector of one of the fluids (permeate of residue).  In terms of mass- and heat-transfer modelling, these aspects are usually neglected, however, for hydrodynamic modelling these must be considered.
texts.text: The membrane may be implemented in the module as a flat or spiral wound sheet or as a bundle of  hollow  fibres.  The  latter  arrangement  is  preferred  as  it  permits  to  develop  the  highest interfacial  areas  per  unit  volume.  An  industrial  hollow  fibre  membrane  module  contains thousands of fibres grouped in the same bundle, using a grid to maintain the bundle structure. A canal is usually placed in the central axis of the module to function as collector of one of the fluids (permeate of residue).  In terms of mass- and heat-transfer modelling, these aspects are usually neglected, however, for hydrodynamic modelling these must be considered.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  driving  force  for  mass-transfer  is  the  gradient  of  chemical  potential.  For  membrane separation, it is conveniently expressed in terms of partial pressure difference. The multicomponent mass-transfer in a membrane module is commonly described as a three step process, as shown in Figure 1. The illustrated species i from the residue side is absorbed by the dense membrane, it then diffuses through the dense-membrane and is finally desorbed at the permeate side. Energy-transfer across the membrane is given by the enthalpy carried by masstransfer, and by conduction through the porous support of fibres. The variation of enthalpy of gases due to the pressure variation is responsible of temperature change and thus of heat transfer.
texts.text: The  driving  force  for  mass-transfer  is  the  gradient  of  chemical  potential.  For  membrane separation, it is conveniently expressed in terms of partial pressure difference. The multicomponent mass-transfer in a membrane module is commonly described as a three step process, as shown in Figure 1. The illustrated species i from the residue side is absorbed by the dense membrane, it then diffuses through the dense-membrane and is finally desorbed at the permeate side. Energy-transfer across the membrane is given by the enthalpy carried by masstransfer, and by conduction through the porous support of fibres. The variation of enthalpy of gases due to the pressure variation is responsible of temperature change and thus of heat transfer.

texts.orig: Figure 1 Schematic representation of a membrane module for gas separation
texts.text: Figure 1 Schematic representation of a membrane module for gas separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The model assumptions are mostly based on the general consensus found in other works [2]-[5]. However, the modelling takes into account the non-isothermal nature of membrane separation of real gases, including phase changes, as well as the variation of membrane permeability, aspects which are often neglected in literature. These assumptions are summarized as follows:
texts.text: The model assumptions are mostly based on the general consensus found in other works [2]-[5]. However, the modelling takes into account the non-isothermal nature of membrane separation of real gases, including phase changes, as well as the variation of membrane permeability, aspects which are often neglected in literature. These assumptions are summarized as follows:

texts.text: Plug flow, no axial dispersion and uniform flow distribution

texts.text: No concentration polarization in gas phases

texts.text: Neglected variation of mass-transfer due to phase changes

texts.text: Compensation of reversible work with viscous dissipation

texts.text: All physicochemical and thermodynamic properties of fluids are obtained from the database and internal  calculations  of  Pro-II.  This  is  advantageous  because  users  can  implement  different

texts.orig: Where the difference in enthalpy between gas sorbed in Henry's low mode and in the gas phase is referred to as Δ HD while the difference in the enthalpy between gas sorbed in the Langmuir sites and in the gas phase is referred to as Δ Hb. EDD and EDH are the activation energies of diffusion associated with the two ways the molecules move through the polymer, sorption and diffusion, respectively. These energetic parameters as well as their corresponding front factors must be supplied by the user.

EDD and EDH are the activation energies of diffusion associated with the two ways the molecules move through the polymer, sorption and diffusion, respectively. These energetic parameters as well as their corresponding front factors must be supplied by the user.

texts.text: The equivalent fibre thermal conductivity is a user supplied parameter.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are typically three flow-modes that are considered to model membrane gas separation modules: Continuous Stirred Tank, Cross-Flow and Parallel-Flow. Schematic representation of these  flow  modes  are  illustrated  in  Figure  2.  The  balance  equations  of  mass,  energy  and momentum for the three modes are presented in Table 1, following the control volume illustrated in Figure 1. Since enthalpy is pressure dependant for real gases, temperature variation cannot be expressed  directly  as  function  of  sensible-heat.  Therefore,  the  temperature  of  both  phases  is implicitly computed through flash calculation. Details are given in Section 3.1.1.
texts.text: There are typically three flow-modes that are considered to model membrane gas separation modules: Continuous Stirred Tank, Cross-Flow and Parallel-Flow. Schematic representation of these  flow  modes  are  illustrated  in  Figure  2.  The  balance  equations  of  mass,  energy  and momentum for the three modes are presented in Table 1, following the control volume illustrated in Figure 1. Since enthalpy is pressure dependant for real gases, temperature variation cannot be expressed  directly  as  function  of  sensible-heat.  Therefore,  the  temperature  of  both  phases  is implicitly computed through flash calculation. Details are given in Section 3.1.1.

texts.label: caption

texts.text: Figure 2. Schematic representation of flow mode for gas permeation modules. a) CSTR, b) Cross-flow, c) Countercurrent

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Free convection: 𝑠 = 𝑆 → (ℎ 𝑟𝑒𝑡 -ℎ 𝑝𝑒𝑟 ) ∑ 𝐽 𝑖 𝑛𝑜𝑐 𝑖=1 +𝑞 = 0

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝑑 𝑑𝑠 𝑃 𝑖𝑛𝑡 = Δ𝑃𝑖𝑛𝑡 𝑆

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝑑 𝑑𝑠 𝑃 𝑖𝑛𝑡 = 𝐹𝑖𝑛𝑡 𝜇 𝑖𝑛 𝐶𝑡𝑜𝑡 64 𝜋 2 𝑟 𝑒 (𝐷𝑚𝑜𝑑 (1- 𝛿 𝑟 𝑒 )) 4

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝑑 𝑑𝑠 𝑃 𝑒𝑥𝑡 = Δ𝑃𝑖𝑛𝑡 𝑆

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝑑 𝑑𝑠 𝑃 𝑒𝑥𝑡 = 𝜇𝑖𝑛 𝐹 𝑖𝑛𝑡 𝐶𝑡𝑜𝑡 8𝑟 𝑒 𝐾𝑘𝑜𝑧 𝜋 2 φ (1φ )𝐷 𝑚𝑜𝑑 4

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Residue through lumen: 𝑠 = 0 → 𝑃 𝑖𝑛𝑡 = 𝑃 𝐹𝑒𝑒𝑑 = 𝑃 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After the version Pro/II-8, User Added Subroutines in are modular and interacts with PRO/II through the modular interfaces. Modular modelling uses Class instances, which are based on object-oriented programming. Details about the definition, use and requirements of UAS modular interfaces and Classes are out of the scope of this document and are given in [9].
texts.text: After the version Pro/II-8, User Added Subroutines in are modular and interacts with PRO/II through the modular interfaces. Modular modelling uses Class instances, which are based on object-oriented programming. Details about the definition, use and requirements of UAS modular interfaces and Classes are out of the scope of this document and are given in [9].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MEMSIC has therefore been adapted to a virtual object-oriented programming, where a module, here called class_ModuleMemb, containing different module types, simulates a class instance having  all  the  parameters  of  a  gas  permeation  simulation.  Within  this  module,  the  model parameters, defined by the developers, and the simulation parameters, assembled in types whose values are user-defined, are created. This module is detailed hereunder:
texts.text: MEMSIC has therefore been adapted to a virtual object-oriented programming, where a module, here called class_ModuleMemb, containing different module types, simulates a class instance having  all  the  parameters  of  a  gas  permeation  simulation.  Within  this  module,  the  model parameters, defined by the developers, and the simulation parameters, assembled in types whose values are user-defined, are created. This module is detailed hereunder:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The different subroutines of MEMSIC are able of communicate between each other by including the class_MembraneModule module, and through a module of global parameters, param. The latter  has  its  version  for  isothermal  calculation,  param_isoth,  and  for  adiabatic  calculation, param_adiabatic.
texts.text: The different subroutines of MEMSIC are able of communicate between each other by including the class_MembraneModule module, and through a module of global parameters, param. The latter  has  its  version  for  isothermal  calculation,  param_isoth,  and  for  adiabatic  calculation, param_adiabatic.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to communicate the UAS with Pro-II simulation environment and Pro-II properties, the following Classes have been used in the source code: class_UAUOP and class_fluid, respectively.
texts.text: In order to communicate the UAS with Pro-II simulation environment and Pro-II properties, the following Classes have been used in the source code: class_UAUOP and class_fluid, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The information related to a fluid is stored in an object called FLUID, which contains all the physicochemical and thermodynamic properties of the fluid. When the characteristics of the fluid vary, for instance when calculating the compositions of permeate, the properties of the fluid change and they have to be recalculated. This is performed through a flash calculation, contained in the module class_fluid, of the fluid. This is of particular advantage for membrane separation simulation since this calculation indicates of any phase change of the fluid.
texts.text: The information related to a fluid is stored in an object called FLUID, which contains all the physicochemical and thermodynamic properties of the fluid. When the characteristics of the fluid vary, for instance when calculating the compositions of permeate, the properties of the fluid change and they have to be recalculated. This is performed through a flash calculation, contained in the module class_fluid, of the fluid. This is of particular advantage for membrane separation simulation since this calculation indicates of any phase change of the fluid.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  order  to  solve  the  different  model  equations,  the  code  has  been  provided  with  numerical libraries containing routines of integration and resolution of equation systems. These routines correspond, amongst others, to the dnqsol.f, a non-linear equation solver, and rkf45_d.f, a RungeKutta method of integration of ordinary differential equation system.
texts.text: In  order  to  solve  the  different  model  equations,  the  code  has  been  provided  with  numerical libraries containing routines of integration and resolution of equation systems. These routines correspond, amongst others, to the dnqsol.f, a non-linear equation solver, and rkf45_d.f, a RungeKutta method of integration of ordinary differential equation system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The heat, mass and momentum balance equations from Table 1 are resolved differently for each flow-mode and for each complexity in modelling, i.e. thermal effects and pressure drop prediction. When the thermal effects are neglected, the energy equations are not resolved. For the case of pressure drop prediction, two hydrodynamic flow options for the permeate stream are allowed, i.e. counter-current and co-current. The algorithm of each flow-mode resolution is described in the following subsections.
texts.text: The heat, mass and momentum balance equations from Table 1 are resolved differently for each flow-mode and for each complexity in modelling, i.e. thermal effects and pressure drop prediction. When the thermal effects are neglected, the energy equations are not resolved. For the case of pressure drop prediction, two hydrodynamic flow options for the permeate stream are allowed, i.e. counter-current and co-current. The algorithm of each flow-mode resolution is described in the following subsections.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The system of 2*NOC+4 algebraic equations exposed in Table 1 for this flow-mode is resolved by using the non-linear equation solver dnqsol.f. The variables that the solver utilizes are the molar fractions (neq = 2*NOC), the temperatures (neq = 2) and the total molar flows (neq = 2) of residue and permeate fluids.
texts.text: The system of 2*NOC+4 algebraic equations exposed in Table 1 for this flow-mode is resolved by using the non-linear equation solver dnqsol.f. The variables that the solver utilizes are the molar fractions (neq = 2*NOC), the temperatures (neq = 2) and the total molar flows (neq = 2) of residue and permeate fluids.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  equations  of  cross-flow  mode  are  divided  in  two  groups:  the  differential  equations corresponding to the residue flow, and the algebraic equations corresponding to the permeate flow. The algebraic equation system is solved using the dnqsol.f routine, being the molar fractions, the temperature and the molar flow of the permeate, the variables of the system. This procedure is performed at each point of integration of the differential equation system, which is integrated using the rkf45_d.f routine. For pressure drop prediction applying the counter-current option, the momentum balance equations are integrated using a conventional initial point integration method. For pressure drop prediction applying the co-current option, a shooting method is used to estimate the permeate pressure drop.
texts.text: The  equations  of  cross-flow  mode  are  divided  in  two  groups:  the  differential  equations corresponding to the residue flow, and the algebraic equations corresponding to the permeate flow. The algebraic equation system is solved using the dnqsol.f routine, being the molar fractions, the temperature and the molar flow of the permeate, the variables of the system. This procedure is performed at each point of integration of the differential equation system, which is integrated using the rkf45_d.f routine. For pressure drop prediction applying the counter-current option, the momentum balance equations are integrated using a conventional initial point integration method. For pressure drop prediction applying the co-current option, a shooting method is used to estimate the permeate pressure drop.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finite differences method with constant step was used to discretize the domain s of the ODE system. Once discretized (equations shown in Appendix A), the system of equations is solved using the dnqsol.f routine. In order to supply the initial point necessary for numerical resolution of  this  non-linear  equation  system,  the  values  of  the  champ  of  variables  are  obtained  by simulating the operation in cross-flow mode. In order to reduce calculation time, the previous initialisation is performed for a grid of 10 points. Then, the results of a 10 points grid are used as initial value for a grid of 20 points. This procedure is repeated sequentially 4 times until a 100 points grid has been reached. This progressive initialisation strategy has proven to be efficient since calculations times were reduced up to a factor of 10.
texts.text: Finite differences method with constant step was used to discretize the domain s of the ODE system. Once discretized (equations shown in Appendix A), the system of equations is solved using the dnqsol.f routine. In order to supply the initial point necessary for numerical resolution of  this  non-linear  equation  system,  the  values  of  the  champ  of  variables  are  obtained  by simulating the operation in cross-flow mode. In order to reduce calculation time, the previous initialisation is performed for a grid of 10 points. Then, the results of a 10 points grid are used as initial value for a grid of 20 points. This procedure is repeated sequentially 4 times until a 100 points grid has been reached. This progressive initialisation strategy has proven to be efficient since calculations times were reduced up to a factor of 10.

texts.label: text

texts.text: The speed of calculation is related to the number of equations that the model need to solve. This amount corresponds to:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The stability of the simulation can be jeopardized by the following aspects:
texts.text: The stability of the simulation can be jeopardized by the following aspects:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Number of stages: it has been optimized to 100 to isothermal calculation and 50 to adiabatic calculation
texts.text: Number of stages: it has been optimized to 100 to isothermal calculation and 50 to adiabatic calculation

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: 1  Internal ProII calculations corresponding to flashes
texts.text: 1  Internal ProII calculations corresponding to flashes

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Permeability  and  composition  of  feed  stream:  too  high  permeability  with  too  low concentrations
texts.text: Permeability  and  composition  of  feed  stream:  too  high  permeability  with  too  low concentrations

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Pressure drop distribution and prediction: distribution is usually the fastest, but if it fails, prediction in CC should be used instead. Pressure drop prediction in CoC is very sensitive to number of fibres.
texts.text: Pressure drop distribution and prediction: distribution is usually the fastest, but if it fails, prediction in CC should be used instead. Pressure drop prediction in CoC is very sensitive to number of fibres.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Export data: after several simulations, some saving conflicts may occur. Thus, this option is to be select when profiles are desired.
texts.text: Export data: after several simulations, some saving conflicts may occur. Thus, this option is to be select when profiles are desired.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Multiphasic feed stream: must be avoided
texts.text: Multiphasic feed stream: must be avoided

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Adiabatic simulation with water: usually of high permeance and low concentration
texts.text: Adiabatic simulation with water: usually of high permeance and low concentration

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Adiabatic and counter-current simulation: it is the slowest simulation. Must be used for precise simulations.
texts.text: Adiabatic and counter-current simulation: it is the slowest simulation. Must be used for precise simulations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The following aspects, amongst others, can be treated to improve the numerical stability, to reduce  the  calculation  time  of  a  simulation  and  to  improve  the  quality  of  the  simulation predictions using the gas permeation UAS:
texts.text: The following aspects, amongst others, can be treated to improve the numerical stability, to reduce  the  calculation  time  of  a  simulation  and  to  improve  the  quality  of  the  simulation predictions using the gas permeation UAS:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Let the user to adjust the numeric tolerance of the integrator (rkf45_d.f subroutine), of the non-linear equation system solver (dnqsol.f subroutine) and to modify the number of stages (Nstages ) of the discretisation. These actions might accelerate simulations.
texts.text: Let the user to adjust the numeric tolerance of the integrator (rkf45_d.f subroutine), of the non-linear equation system solver (dnqsol.f subroutine) and to modify the number of stages (Nstages ) of the discretisation. These actions might accelerate simulations.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Regarding the adiabatic simulations, future developers might limit the choice of thermodynamic methods to a simple one, e.g. SRK or PR.  Then, by means of analytic mathematical procedures, temperature variation might be explicitly expressed, thus reducing considerably the number of calculations.
texts.text: Regarding the adiabatic simulations, future developers might limit the choice of thermodynamic methods to a simple one, e.g. SRK or PR.  Then, by means of analytic mathematical procedures, temperature variation might be explicitly expressed, thus reducing considerably the number of calculations.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · As  included  in  Appendix  B,  in  some  cases  and  under  some  conditions,  concentration polarization may affect membrane module performances. Therefore, it would be worth to introduce an option taking into account this phenomenon.
texts.text: As  included  in  Appendix  B,  in  some  cases  and  under  some  conditions,  concentration polarization may affect membrane module performances. Therefore, it would be worth to introduce an option taking into account this phenomenon.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This section contains the discretization of the differential equation system describing an adiabatic membrane  module  operating  in  counter-current  flow  and  free  convection  for  permeate.  The differential equations to be discretised are presented as follows (same equations included in Table 1):
texts.text: This section contains the discretization of the differential equation system describing an adiabatic membrane  module  operating  in  counter-current  flow  and  free  convection  for  permeate.  The differential equations to be discretised are presented as follows (same equations included in Table 1):

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝑑 𝑑𝑠 (𝑦 𝑖 𝑟𝑒𝑡 ) = 1 𝐹 𝑟𝑒𝑡 (𝐽𝑖 -𝑦𝑖 𝑟𝑒𝑡 ∑𝐽𝑖 𝑖 ) (A.1) 𝑑 𝑑𝑠 (𝐹 𝑟𝑒𝑡 ) = - ∑ 𝐽𝑖 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 (A.2) 𝑑 𝑑𝑠 (𝑃 𝑟𝑒𝑡 ) = Ρ ret (A.3) 𝑑 𝑑𝑠 (𝐻 𝑟𝑒𝑡 ) = - (𝑞 + 𝐻 𝑟𝑒𝑡 ∑𝐽𝑖 𝑛𝑜𝑐 𝑖=1 ) (A.4) 𝑑 𝑑𝑠 (𝑦 𝑖 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 ) = -1 𝐹 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 (𝐽𝑖 -𝑦 𝑖 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 ∑𝐽𝑖 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 ) (A.5) 𝑑 𝑑𝑠 (𝐹 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 ) = - ∑ 𝐽 𝑖 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 (A.6) 𝑑 𝑑𝑠 (𝑃 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 ) = Ρ perm (A.7) 𝑑 𝑑𝑠 (𝐻 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 ) = - (𝑞 + 𝐻 𝑟𝑒𝑡 ∑𝐽𝑖 𝑛𝑜𝑐 ) (A.8)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The discretisation of the differential system is performed using the forward differentiation method for all variables of the residue stream and for the permeate pressure. The backward differentiation method is then applied to the rest of variables, i.e. y i perm , F perm and H perm . For a grid of j=N points and Δ s = S/N, the equations are as follows:
texts.text: The discretisation of the differential system is performed using the forward differentiation method for all variables of the residue stream and for the permeate pressure. The backward differentiation method is then applied to the rest of variables, i.e. y i perm , F perm and H perm . For a grid of j=N points and Δ s = S/N, the equations are as follows:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝑦 𝑖,1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 -𝑦𝑖 𝐹𝐸𝐸𝐷 -∆𝑠( 1 𝐹 1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 (𝑦𝑖,1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 ∑𝐽𝑖,1 𝑁𝑂𝐶 ) - 𝐽 𝑖,1 ) = 0

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝑖 (A.9) 𝐹 1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 -𝐹 𝐹𝐸𝐸𝐷 +∆𝑠 ∑ 𝐽𝑖,1 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 = 0 (A.10) 𝑃 1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 -𝑃 𝐹𝐸𝐸𝐷 - ∆𝑠Ρ 1 ret = 0 (A.11)

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝐻1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 -𝐻 𝐹𝐸𝐸𝐷 + ∆𝑠 (𝑞1 + 𝐻1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 𝐹 1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 ∑𝐽𝑖,1 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 ) = 0 (A.12) 𝑦 𝑖,2 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 -𝑦 𝑖,1 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 -∆𝑠( 1 𝐹 1 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 (𝑦 𝑖,1 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 ∑𝐽𝑖,1 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 ) - 𝐽 𝑖,1 ) = 0 (A.13) 𝐹 2 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 -𝐹 1 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 +∆𝑠 ∑ 𝐽𝑖,1 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 = 0 (A.14) 𝑃 1 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 -𝑃 𝑑𝑜𝑤𝑛 = 0 (A.15) 𝐻 2 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 -𝐻1 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 +∆𝑠(𝑞1 + 𝐻1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 𝐹 1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 ∑𝐽𝑖,1 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 ) = 0 (A.16)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the stages j=2 to j=N:
texts.text: For the stages j=2 to j=N:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝑦 𝑖,𝑗 𝑟𝑒𝑡 -𝑦𝑖,𝑗-1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 -∆𝑠( 1 𝐹 𝑗 𝑟𝑒𝑡 (𝑦𝑖,𝑗 𝑟𝑒𝑡 ∑𝐽𝑖,𝑗 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 ) - 𝐽 𝑖,𝑗 ) = 0

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: (A.17) 𝐹 𝑗 𝑟𝑒𝑡 -𝐹 𝑗-1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 +∆𝑠 ∑ 𝐽𝑖,𝑗 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 = 0 (A.18) 𝑃 𝑗 𝑟𝑒𝑡 -𝑃 𝑗-1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 -∆𝑠Ρ 𝑗 ret = 0 (A.19) 𝐻 𝑗 𝑟𝑒𝑡 -𝐻𝑗-1 𝑟𝑒𝑡 +∆𝑠(𝑞𝑗 + 𝐻 𝑗 𝑟𝑒𝑡 𝐹 𝑗 𝑟𝑒𝑡 ∑𝐽𝑖,𝑗 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 ) = 0 (A.20) 𝑃 𝑗 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 -𝑃 𝑗-1 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 -∆𝑠Ρ 𝑗 perm = 0 (A.21)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the stages j=2 to j=N-1:
texts.text: For the stages j=2 to j=N-1:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝑦 𝑖,𝑗+1 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 -𝑦 𝑖,𝑗 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 -∆𝑠( 1 𝐹 𝑗 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 (𝑦 𝑖,𝑗 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 ∑𝐽𝑖,𝑗 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 ) - 𝐽 𝑖,𝑗 ) = 0

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝐹 𝑗+1 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 -𝐹 𝑗 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 +∆𝑠 ∑ 𝐽𝑖,𝑗 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 = 0

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝐻 𝑗+1 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 -𝐻 𝑗 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 +∆𝑠(𝑞𝑗 + 𝐻 𝑗 𝑟𝑒𝑡 𝐹 𝑗 𝑟𝑒𝑡 ∑𝐽𝑖,𝑗 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 ) = 0

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝑦 𝑖,𝑁 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 𝐽 𝑖,𝑁 ∑ 𝐽 𝑖,𝑁 𝑁𝑂𝐶 = 0

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝐹 𝑁 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 -∆𝑠 ∑ 𝐽𝑖,𝑁 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 = 0

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ( 𝐻𝑁 𝑟𝑒𝑡 𝐹 𝑁 𝑟𝑒𝑡 -𝐻 𝑁 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 𝐹 𝑁 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑚 ) ∑ 𝐽𝑖,𝑗 𝑁𝑂𝐶 𝑖 +𝑞𝑗 = 0 (A.27)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The final system of (2*NOC + 6)*N equations is solved using the dnqsol.f routine. Cross-flow mode is simulated to supply the initial point. In order to reduce calculation time, the previous initialisation is performed for a grid of 10 points. Then, the results of a 10 points grid are used as initial value for a grid of 20 points (the additional internal points are interpolated using a spline routine). This procedure is repeated sequentially 3 times until a 50 points grid has been reached  for adiabatic  simulations, and  4  times  until  a  100  points  grid  has  been  reached  for isothermal simulations.
texts.text: The final system of (2*NOC + 6)*N equations is solved using the dnqsol.f routine. Cross-flow mode is simulated to supply the initial point. In order to reduce calculation time, the previous initialisation is performed for a grid of 10 points. Then, the results of a 10 points grid are used as initial value for a grid of 20 points (the additional internal points are interpolated using a spline routine). This procedure is repeated sequentially 3 times until a 50 points grid has been reached  for adiabatic  simulations, and  4  times  until  a  100  points  grid  has  been  reached  for isothermal simulations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The resistance to the molar flux from the residue bulk to the permeate bulk can be seen as a sum of local resistances corresponding to the residue, the fibre and the permeate. For typical intrinsic permeability values of membranes for natural gas treatment and by setting the permeate pressure to 1 bar, we can neglect the mass-transfer resistance due to the permeate. However, since diffusion coefficients in gas phase increase almost proportionally with pressure, the mass-transfer resistance of the residue might impact the transfer performance of the membrane module. Therefore, the transmembrane flux can be roughly estimated as follows:
texts.text: The resistance to the molar flux from the residue bulk to the permeate bulk can be seen as a sum of local resistances corresponding to the residue, the fibre and the permeate. For typical intrinsic permeability values of membranes for natural gas treatment and by setting the permeate pressure to 1 bar, we can neglect the mass-transfer resistance due to the permeate. However, since diffusion coefficients in gas phase increase almost proportionally with pressure, the mass-transfer resistance of the residue might impact the transfer performance of the membrane module. Therefore, the transmembrane flux can be roughly estimated as follows:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝐽 𝑖 = 𝑃𝑒𝑒𝑞 (𝑃 𝑖 𝑟𝑒𝑡 -𝑃 𝑖 𝑝𝑒𝑟 ) = 𝐾(𝐶 𝑖 𝑟𝑒𝑠 -𝐶 𝑖 𝑝𝑒𝑟 ) (A.28)

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 1 𝑃𝑒𝑒𝑞 = 𝑅𝑇 𝐾 = 1 𝑃𝑒𝑑𝑒𝑛𝑠𝑒 + 1 𝑃𝑒𝑟𝑒𝑠 = 1 𝑃𝑒𝑑𝑒𝑛𝑠𝑒 + 𝑅𝑇 𝑘𝑟𝑒𝑠 (A.29)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Peeq, Pedense and Peres are the equivalent permeance, the permeance of the dense membrane and the permeance of the residue. For laminar and fully developed flow through a pipe, Sherwood number  can  be  roughly  set  to  4.  At  1  bar  of  pressure,  the  order  of  magnitude  of  diffusion coefficients is of 10 -5 ms -1 . Therefore, the mass-transfer coefficient related to the residue can be estimated as:
texts.text: Peeq, Pedense and Peres are the equivalent permeance, the permeance of the dense membrane and the permeance of the residue. For laminar and fully developed flow through a pipe, Sherwood number  can  be  roughly  set  to  4.  At  1  bar  of  pressure,  the  order  of  magnitude  of  diffusion coefficients is of 10 -5 ms -1 . Therefore, the mass-transfer coefficient related to the residue can be estimated as:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: 𝑘𝑟𝑒𝑠 = 𝑆ℎ · 𝐷𝑖 𝑑ℎ = 𝑆ℎ · 𝐷𝑖,0 𝑃 · 𝑑ℎ = 4 · 10 -5 𝑃 · 𝑑ℎ (A.30)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For  a  binary  mixture  having  an  intrinsic  permeability  of  20  barrer  and  a  selectivity  4,  the influence of the concentration polarisation on the equivalent permeance (Equation A.29) with the residue pressure is shown in Figure 4. A similar figure, Figure 5, has been drawn for a system having an intrinsic permeability of 150 barrer and a selectivity 30.
texts.text: For  a  binary  mixture  having  an  intrinsic  permeability  of  20  barrer  and  a  selectivity  4,  the influence of the concentration polarisation on the equivalent permeance (Equation A.29) with the residue pressure is shown in Figure 4. A similar figure, Figure 5, has been drawn for a system having an intrinsic permeability of 150 barrer and a selectivity 30.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 4 Ratio of the equivalent permeability (left) and selectivity (right) values with and without concentration polarization as function of the residue (feed) pressure. Pedense = 20 barrers, S = 4.
texts.text: Figure 4 Ratio of the equivalent permeability (left) and selectivity (right) values with and without concentration polarization as function of the residue (feed) pressure. Pedense = 20 barrers, S = 4.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 5 Ratio of the equivalent permeability (left) and selectivity (right) values with and without concentration polarization as function of the residue (feed) pressure. Pedense = 150 barrers, S = 30.
texts.text: Figure 5 Ratio of the equivalent permeability (left) and selectivity (right) values with and without concentration polarization as function of the residue (feed) pressure. Pedense = 150 barrers, S = 30.

table_cells.text: Pressure drop for permeate is computed assuming parallel co- current flow. Prediction is performed for hollow fibre membranes, using Poiseuille's law for the fluid through lumen ( P int ), and Happel consideration for the fluid through shell ( P ext ). For residue flowing through shell, boundary conditions must be flipped, i.e. P int = P perm and P ext = P Feed For isothermal simulation, the viscosity and the compressibility factor of both residue and permeate are supposed to not be concentration

Equations for Counter-Current and permeate free flow. For isothermal simulation, the viscosity and the compressibility factor of both residue and permeate are supposed to not be concentration dependant. For co-current resolution, the right- hand term of the mass and energy differential equations related to the permeate, must be multiplied by -1. The boundary conditions must be adapted to begin at s=0. For residue flowing through shell, boundary conditions must be flipped, i.e. P = P perm and P = P Feed

grid.text: Pressure drop for permeate is computed assuming parallel co- current flow. Prediction is performed for hollow fibre membranes, using Poiseuille's law for the fluid through lumen ( P int ), and Happel consideration for the fluid through shell ( P ext ). For residue flowing through shell, boundary conditions must be flipped, i.e. P int = P perm and P ext = P Feed For isothermal simulation, the viscosity and the compressibility factor of both residue and permeate are supposed to not be concentration

Equations for Counter-Current and permeate free flow. For isothermal simulation, the viscosity and the compressibility factor of both residue and permeate are supposed to not be concentration dependant. For co-current resolution, the right- hand term of the mass and energy differential equations related to the permeate, must be multiplied by -1. The boundary conditions must be adapted to begin at s=0. For residue flowing through shell, boundary conditions must be flipped, i.e. P = P perm and P = P Feed
